chunks.content:
THE TELL-TALE HEART.
True!—nervous—very, very dreadfully nervous I had been and am; but why will you say that
I am mad? The disease had sharpened my senses—not destroyed—not dulled them. Above
all was the sense of hearing acute. I heard all things in the heaven and in the earth. I heard
many things in hell. How, then, am I mad? Hearken! and observe how healthily—how
calmly I can tell you the whole story.
It is impossible to say how first the idea entered my brain; but once conceived, it haunted
me day and night. Object there was none. Passion there was none. I loved the old man. He
had never wronged me. He had never given me insult. For his gold I had no desire. I think it
was his eye! yes, it was this! He had the eye of a vulture—a pale blue eye, with a film over it.
Whenever it fell upon me, my blood ran cold; and so by degrees—very gradually—I made up
my mind to take the life of the old man, and thus rid myself of the eye forever.
Now this is the point. You fancy me mad. Madmen know

this is the point. You fancy me mad. Madmen know nothing. But you should have seen
me. You should have seen how wisely I proceeded—with what caution—with what
foresight—with what dissimulation I went to work! I was never kinder to the old man than
during the whole week before I killed him. And every night, about midnight, I turned the
latch of his door and opened it—oh, so gently! And then, when I had made an opening
sufficient for my head, I put in a dark lantern, all closed, closed, that no light shone out,
and then I thrust in my head. Oh, you would have laughed to see how cunningly I thrust it in!
I moved it slowly—very, very slowly, so that I might not disturb the old man’s sleep. It took
me an hour to place my whole head within the opening so far that I could see him as he lay
upon his bed. Ha!—would a madman have been so wise as this? And then, when my head
was well in the room, I undid the lantern cautiously—oh, so cautiously—cautiously (for the
hinges creaked)—I undid it just

(for the
hinges creaked)—I undid it just so much that a single thin ray fell upon the vulture eye. And
this I did for seven long nights—every night just at midnight—but I found the eye always
closed; and so it was impossible to do the work; for it was not the old man who vexed me,
but his Evil Eye. And every morning, when the day broke, I went boldly into the chamber,
and spoke courageously to him, calling him by name in a hearty tone, and inquiring how he
has passed the night. So you see he would have been a very profound old man, indeed, to
suspect that every night, just at twelve, I looked in upon him while he slept.
Upon the eighth night I was more than usually cautious in opening the door. A watch’s
minute hand moves more quickly than did mine. Never before that night had I felt the extent
of my own powers—of my sagacity. I could scarcely contain my feelings of triumph. To think
that there I was, opening the door, little by little, and he not even to dream of my secret
deeds or

and he not even to dream of my secret
deeds or thoughts. I fairly chuckled at the idea; and perhaps he heard me; for he moved on
the bed suddenly, as if startled. Now you may think that I drew back—but no. His room was

as black as pitch with the thick darkness, (for the shutters were close fastened, through
fear of robbers,) and so I knew that he could not see the opening of the door, and I kept
pushing it on steadily, steadily.
I had my head in, and was about to open the lantern, when my thumb slipped upon the tin
fastening, and the old man sprang up in bed, crying out—“Who’s there?”
I kept quite still and said nothing. For a whole hour I did not move a muscle, and in the
meantime I did not hear him lie down. He was still sitting up in the bed listening;—just as I
have done, night after night, hearkening to the death watches in the wall.
Presently I heard a slight groan, and I knew it was the groan of mortal terror. It was not a
groan of pain or of grief—oh, no!—it was the low

groan of pain or of grief—oh, no!—it was the low stifled sound that arises from the bottom
of the soul when overcharged with awe. I knew the sound well. Many a night, just at
midnight, when all the world slept, it has welled up from my own bosom, deepening, with
its dreadful echo, the terrors that distracted me. I say I knew it well. I knew what the old
man felt, and pitied him, although I chuckled at heart. I knew that he had been lying awake
ever since the first slight noise, when he had turned in the bed. His fears had been ever
since growing upon him. He had been trying to fancy them causeless, but could not. He
had been saying to himself—“It is nothing but the wind in the chimney—it is only a mouse
crossing the floor,” or “It is merely a cricket which has made a single chirp.” Yes, he had
been trying to comfort himself with these suppositions: but he had found all in vain. All in
vain; because Death, in approaching him had stalked with his black shadow before him,
and enveloped

with his black shadow before him,
and enveloped the victim. And it was the mournful influence of the unperceived shadow
that caused him to feel—although he neither saw nor heard—to feel the presence of my
head within the room.
When I had waited a long time, very patiently, without hearing him lie down, I resolved to
open a little—a very, very little crevice in the lantern. So I opened it—you cannot imagine
how stealthily, stealthily—until, at length a simple dim ray, like the thread of the spider,
shot from out the crevice and fell full upon the vulture eye.
It was open—wide, wide open—and I grew furious as I gazed upon it. I saw it with perfect
distinctness—all a dull blue, with a hideous veil over it that chilled the very marrow in my
bones; but I could see nothing else of the old man’s face or person: for I had directed the
ray as if by instinct, precisely upon the damned spot.
And have I not told you that what you mistake for madness is but over-acuteness of the
sense?—now, I say,

is but over-acuteness of the
sense?—now, I say, there came to my ears a low, dull, quick sound, such as a watch makes
when enveloped in cotton. I knew that sound well, too. It was the beating of the old man’s
heart. It increased my fury, as the beating of a drum stimulates the soldier into courage.

But even yet I refrained and kept still. I scarcely breathed. I held the lantern motionless. I
tried how steadily I could maintain the ray upon the eye. Meantime the hellish tattoo of the
heart increased. It grew quicker and quicker, and louder and louder every instant. The old
man’s terror must have been extreme! It grew louder, I say, louder every moment!—do you
mark me well? I have told you that I am nervous: so I am. And now at the dead hour of the
night, amid the dreadful silence of that old house, so strange a noise as this excited me to
uncontrollable terror. Yet, for some minutes longer I refrained and stood still. But the
beating grew louder, louder! I thought the heart must burst.

louder, louder! I thought the heart must burst. And now a new anxiety seized
me—the sound would be heard by a neighbour! The old man’s hour had come! With a loud
yell, I threw open the lantern and leaped into the room. He shrieked once—once only. In an
instant I dragged him to the floor, and pulled the heavy bed over him. I then smiled gaily, to
find the deed so far done. But, for many minutes, the heart beat on with a muffled sound.
This, however, did not vex me; it would not be heard through the wall. At length it ceased.
The old man was dead. I removed the bed and examined the corpse. Yes, he was stone,
stone dead. I placed my hand upon the heart and held it there many minutes. There was no
pulsation. He was stone dead. His eye would trouble me no more.
If still you think me mad, you will think so no longer when I describe the wise precautions I
took for the concealment of the body. The night waned, and I worked hastily, but in silence.
First of all I dismembered the corpse. I cut

First of all I dismembered the corpse. I cut off the head and the arms and the legs.
I then took up three planks from the flooring of the chamber, and deposited all between the
scantlings. I then replaced the boards so cleverly, so cunningly, that no human eye—not
even his—could have detected any thing wrong. There was nothing to wash out—no stain
of any kind—no blood-spot whatever. I had been too wary for that. A tub had caught all—
ha! ha!
When I had made an end of these labors, it was four o’clock—still dark as midnight. As the
bell sounded the hour, there came a knocking at the street door. I went down to open it with
a light heart,—for what had I now to fear? There entered three men, who introduced
themselves, with perfect suavity, as officers of the police. A shriek had been heard by a
neighbour during the night; suspicion of foul play had been aroused; information had been
lodged at the police office, and they (the officers) had been deputed to search the
premises.
I smiled,—for

deputed to search the
premises.
I smiled,—for what had I to fear? I bade the gentlemen welcome. The shriek, I said, was my
own in a dream. The old man, I mentioned, was absent in the country. I took my visitors all
over the house. I bade them search—search well. I led them, at length, to his chamber. I
showed them his treasures, secure, undisturbed. In the enthusiasm of my confidence, I
brought chairs into the room, and desired them here to rest from their fatigues, while I

myself, in the wild audacity of my perfect triumph, placed my own seat upon the very spot
beneath which reposed the corpse of the victim.
The officers were satisfied. My manner had convinced them. I was singularly at ease. They
sat, and while I answered cheerily, they chatted of familiar things. But, ere long, I felt myself
getting pale and wished them gone. My head ached, and I fancied a ringing in my ears: but
still they sat and still chatted. The ringing became more distinct:—it continued and became
more

more distinct:—it continued and became
more distinct: I talked more freely to get rid of the feeling: but it continued and gained
definiteness—until, at length, I found that the noise was not within my ears.
No doubt I now grew very pale;—but I talked more fluently, and with a heightened voice. Yet
the sound increased—and what could I do? It was a low, dull, quick sound—much such a
sound as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton. I gasped for breath—and yet the
officers heard it not. I talked more quickly—more vehemently; but the noise steadily
increased. I arose and argued about trifles, in a high key and with violent gesticulations; but
the noise steadily increased. Why would they not be gone? I paced the floor to and fro with
heavy strides, as if excited to fury by the observations of the men—but the noise steadily
increased. Oh God! what could I do? I foamed—I raved—I swore! I swung the chair upon
which I had been sitting, and grated it upon the boards, but the noise arose over all

it upon the boards, but the noise arose over all and
continually increased. It grew louder—louder—louder! And still the men chatted
pleasantly, and smiled. Was it possible they heard not? Almighty God!—no, no! They
heard!—they suspected!—they knew!—they were making a mockery of my horror!—this I
thought, and this I think. But anything was better than this agony! Anything was more
tolerable than this derision! I could bear those hypocritical smiles no longer! I felt that I
must scream or die! and now—again!—hark! louder! louder! louder! louder!
“Villains!” I shrieked, “dissemble no more! I admit the deed!—tear up the planks!—here,
here!—It is the beating of his hideous heart!”

Meridian Technologies, Inc.
Information Security Policy
Document ID: SEC-POL-2025-001 | Version 4.2 | Effective Date: January 1, 2025
Classification: Internal — All Employees | Owner: Chief Information Security Officer
Next Review Date: July 1, 2025 | Approved by: Executive Leadership Team
1. Purpose
The purpose of this Information Security Policy is to establish the requirements and responsibilities for
protecting Meridian Technologies' information assets, systems, and data. This policy defines the
minimum security standards that all employees, contractors, and third-party partners must adhere to
when accessing, processing, storing, or transmitting company information. The policy is designed to
protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information in accordance with industry best
practices and applicable regulatory requirements.
This document supersedes all prior versions of the Information Security Policy and incorporates
updates reflecting changes in the threat

updates reflecting changes in the threat landscape, organizational structure, and regulatory
environment observed during FY2024. All personnel are expected to read this document in its entirety
and acknowledge their understanding through the annual compliance training portal.
2. Scope
This policy applies to all Meridian Technologies employees, temporary workers, interns, contractors,
consultants, and third-party service providers who have access to company information systems or
data. It covers all information assets regardless of format, including but not limited to electronic data,
paper records, intellectual property, customer data, financial records, and employee personal
information. The policy applies to all computing environments operated or managed by the company,
including on-premises data centers, cloud infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP), employee workstations,
mobile devices, and remote access systems.
Business units that process data subject to additional regulatory

process data subject to additional regulatory requirements (e.g., HIPAA, PCI-DSS,
GDPR) must comply with both this policy and the applicable supplementary policies maintained by the
compliance team. In the event of a conflict between this policy and a regulatory-specific policy, the
more restrictive requirement applies.
3. Data Classification
All company information must be classified according to the following four-tier system. Data owners are
responsible for assigning the appropriate classification level at the time of creation or acquisition. The
classification determines the minimum security controls required for storage, transmission, and access.
Level Label Description Examples
1 Public No restrictions on disclosure Marketing materials, press releases

2 Internal For employee use only Org charts, internal memos, policies
3 Confidential Restricted to authorized personnel Customer data, financial reports, contracts
4 Highly Confidential Strict need-to-know access Trade secrets, M&A

Strict need-to-know access Trade secrets, M&A plans, encryption keys
Information classified at Level 3 or above must be encrypted at rest and in transit using AES-256 or an
equivalent algorithm approved by the security team. Access to Level 4 data requires explicit written
authorization from both the data owner and the CISO. Reclassification requests must be submitted
through the data governance portal and are subject to review by the Information Security team within 5
business days.
4. Access Control
Access to information systems and data must follow the principle of least privilege. Users shall be
granted the minimum level of access necessary to perform their job functions. All access requests must
be submitted through the IT Service Management portal and approved by the employee's direct
manager and the system owner. Access reviews are conducted quarterly by system owners to verify
that all granted permissions remain appropriate. Accounts that have not been used for 60 consecutive

that have not been used for 60 consecutive
days will be automatically disabled and require reactivation through the standard request process.
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is required for all remote access connections, cloud service
administrator accounts, and systems processing Level 3 or Level 4 data. Approved MFA methods
include hardware security keys (FIDO2), time-based one-time passwords (TOTP) via an approved
authenticator application, and push notifications through the company-managed mobile device
management platform. SMS-based one-time passwords are not permitted as a primary MFA factor due
to known vulnerabilities in the SS7 protocol.
Service accounts and machine identities must be managed through the centralized secrets
management platform. Shared or generic accounts are prohibited except where a documented
technical exception has been approved by the CISO. All privileged access must be brokered through
the privileged access management (PAM) system, and sessions must be

management (PAM) system, and sessions must be recorded for audit purposes.
5. Password Policy
All user-created passwords must meet the following minimum requirements: a length of at least 14
characters, inclusion of at least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, one numeric digit, and one
special character from the set (!@#$%^&*). Passwords must not contain the user's name, username, or
any dictionary word longer than four characters. The system will reject any password that has appeared
in known breach databases, as checked against the HaveIBeenPwned API at the time of creation.
Password rotation is required every 90 days for all standard user accounts. Privileged accounts,
including domain administrator and database administrator credentials, must rotate passwords every
30 days. Upon rotation, the new password must differ from the previous 12 passwords stored in the
credential history. Employees who fail to rotate their password within the required window will have
their

within the required window will have
their account locked after a 7-day grace period, and must contact the IT helpdesk to regain access.
The use of a company-approved password manager is strongly encouraged for all employees. The IT
department provides enterprise licenses for the approved password manager, and training on its use is
included in the annual security awareness program. Employees must not store passwords in plaintext

files, browser autofill systems not managed by IT, sticky notes, or shared documents. Where
technically feasible, passwordless authentication methods (e.g., FIDO2 security keys, certificate-based
authentication) should be preferred over traditional passwords.
6. Network Security
All network traffic between security zones must pass through next-generation firewalls with intrusion
prevention capabilities enabled. The network is segmented into distinct zones including a DMZ for
public-facing services, a production zone for application workloads, a management

zone for application workloads, a management zone for
administrative access, and a restricted zone for systems processing highly confidential data. Inter-zone
traffic is permitted only through explicitly defined firewall rules that are reviewed monthly by the network
security team.
All employees connecting remotely must use the company-provided VPN client with split tunneling
disabled. The VPN gateway enforces MFA and device health checks before granting access. Wireless
networks in company offices use WPA3-Enterprise with RADIUS authentication. Guest wireless
networks are logically isolated from the corporate network and provide internet access only. Network
traffic is monitored continuously by the Security Operations Center (SOC) using a SIEM platform that
correlates events from firewalls, endpoint detection systems, DNS logs, and cloud security telemetry.
7. Incident Response
All employees are required to report suspected security incidents immediately through the designated

incidents immediately through the designated
reporting channels: the #security-incidents Slack channel, the security@meridiantech.com email alias,
or by calling the 24/7 SOC hotline at extension 4400. The incident response team operates under a
tiered severity classification system ranging from SEV-4 (low impact, informational) to SEV-1 (critical,
active breach with data exfiltration or system compromise). SEV-1 and SEV-2 incidents trigger
automatic escalation to the CISO and executive leadership within 30 minutes of classification.
The incident response lifecycle follows the NIST SP 800-61 framework: Preparation, Detection and
Analysis, Containment Eradication and Recovery, and Post-Incident Activity. A post-incident review is
mandatory for all SEV-1 through SEV-3 incidents and must be completed within 5 business days of
incident closure. Findings from post-incident reviews are tracked in the risk register and inform updates
to security controls, monitoring rules, and this policy. The

controls, monitoring rules, and this policy. The company conducts tabletop exercises
quarterly and a full-scale incident simulation annually to test the readiness of the incident response
team.
8. Endpoint Security
All company-issued endpoints must run the approved endpoint detection and response (EDR) agent at
all times. The EDR agent is configured to perform real-time behavioral analysis, file integrity monitoring,
and automatic isolation of endpoints exhibiting indicators of compromise. Operating system and
application patches must be applied within 14 calendar days of release for standard updates and within
72 hours for critical security patches rated CVSS 9.0 or above. Full disk encryption must be enabled on
all laptops and portable storage devices.
9. Compliance and Enforcement

Compliance with this policy is mandatory for all personnel within scope. The Information Security team
conducts periodic audits to verify adherence and may perform unannounced assessments of systems
and

perform unannounced assessments of systems
and processes. Violations of this policy may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination
of employment or contract, and may be referred to law enforcement where applicable. Employees are
required to complete annual security awareness training and acknowledge this policy annually. The
CISO is responsible for maintaining this policy and ensuring it remains aligned with current threats,
business requirements, and regulatory obligations.
Document revision history, exception request procedures, and supplementary technical standards are maintained separately in
the Meridian Technologies Security Standards Library (SSL) accessible via the company intranet at
https://security.internal.meridiantech.com/ssl.

Acme Corp
Quarterly Earnings Report — Q4 Fiscal Year 2024
Prepared by the Office of the Chief Financial Officer | Released January 28, 2025
Classification: Public — Approved for External Distribution
Executive Summary
Acme Corp closed the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2024 with strong momentum across all business
segments. The enterprise software division continued to outperform internal targets, while the managed
services group stabilized after the restructuring completed in Q3. Total headcount reached 1,247
full-time employees, up from 1,183 at the end of Q3, reflecting targeted hiring in the engineering and
customer success organizations. The board of directors approved a $2.1M allocation for R&D;
expansion in Q1 2025, focused on the Acme Sentinel product line.
Overall, the leadership team views Q4 as a validation of the strategic pivot toward mid-market
enterprise accounts that began in early 2023. Gross margins improved by 3.2 percentage points
compared to the prior quarter, and

points
compared to the prior quarter, and operating expenses remained within the guidance range
communicated to shareholders in October. The company ended the quarter with $18.7M in cash and
cash equivalents, providing a healthy runway for the planned geographic expansion into EMEA markets
during the first half of 2025.
Financial Highlights
Q4 revenue reached $5M, representing a 17% increase compared to Q4 of the prior fiscal year and a
6% sequential increase over Q3. This figure exceeded the internal forecast of $4.7M and reflects
strength in both new bookings and expansion revenue from existing accounts. Subscription revenue
accounted for $3.8M of the total, while professional services contributed $0.9M. The remaining $0.3M
was attributable to one-time implementation fees associated with two large enterprise deployments
completed in December.
Metric Q4 FY2024 Q3 FY2024 Q4 FY2023
Total Revenue $5.0M $4.7M $4.3M
Subscription Revenue $3.8M $3.5M $3.1M
Professional Services $0.9M $0.9M

$3.5M $3.1M
Professional Services $0.9M $0.9M $0.8M
Gross Margin 71.4% 68.2% 65.9%
Operating Expenses $3.1M $3.0M $2.8M
EBITDA $0.8M $0.5M $0.3M
Net Income (Loss) $0.4M $0.1M ($0.2M)
Cost of revenue decreased as a percentage of total revenue, driven by improved hosting efficiency
following the migration of 62% of workloads to a containerized architecture in Q3. The company
expects to complete the remaining migration by the end of Q1 2025, which should yield an additional

1.5-2.0 percentage point improvement in gross margins. Sales and marketing expenses increased by
$0.15M quarter-over-quarter due to the annual user conference held in November and a new digital
advertising campaign targeting the healthcare vertical.
Customer Metrics
Acme Corp added 38 net new customers during Q4, bringing the total active customer count to 412.
The company's customer growth rate reached 12% year-over-year, accelerating from the 9% growth
rate observed in Q3. This acceleration is attributed to the

in Q3. This acceleration is attributed to the launch of the Acme Starter tier in September,
which lowered the barrier to entry for small and mid-size businesses. Of the 38 new customers, 14
entered through the Starter tier and are expected to expand into higher-value plans within 6 to 12
months based on historical conversion patterns.
Customer Metric Q4 FY2024 Q4 FY2023 Change
Total Active Customers 412 368 +12%
Net New Customers (Quarter) 38 27 +41%
Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) $16.2M $13.4M +21%
Net Revenue Retention 113% 108% +5 pts
Average Contract Value $39.3K $36.4K +8%
Customer Churn Rate 1.8% 2.4% -0.6 pts
Net revenue retention of 113% indicates that existing customers are expanding their usage at a healthy
rate. The customer success team completed onboarding for all Q3 cohort accounts within the quarter,
maintaining the 45-day average time-to-value metric. The top five accounts by ARR contributed $2.4M
in combined annual recurring revenue, representing 14.8% of total ARR.

revenue, representing 14.8% of total ARR. The company continues to
reduce concentration risk, as this figure was 19.1% at the end of Q4 FY2023.
Product and Engineering Update
The engineering organization shipped 4 major releases during Q4, including Acme Sentinel v2.3 with
real-time anomaly detection, the redesigned administration console, a SOC 2 Type II compliance
reporting module, and the new REST API v3.0. The platform processed an average of 2.8 billion events
per day during Q4, up from 2.1 billion in Q3, with 99.97% uptime across all production environments. A
total of 147 bugs were resolved during the quarter, and the engineering team reduced the average
incident response time from 14 minutes to 9 minutes.
Looking ahead, the product roadmap for Q1 2025 includes native integrations with Salesforce and
ServiceNow, a self-service provisioning portal for channel partners, and early access to the Acme
Copilot AI assistant feature. The team is also evaluating a potential migration

The team is also evaluating a potential migration from PostgreSQL to
CockroachDB for the multi-tenant metadata store to improve horizontal scalability.
Outlook and Forward Guidance
Management is issuing Q1 FY2025 revenue guidance of $5.2M to $5.5M, reflecting anticipated
seasonal softness in professional services offset by continued subscription growth. Full-year FY2025

revenue is expected to fall in the range of $22M to $24M, representing 20-30% annual growth. The
company plans to invest heavily in go-to-market capacity during H1, with 15-20 planned hires across
sales, solutions engineering, and marketing. The EMEA expansion will begin with a small team based
in London, targeting the UK and DACH regions initially.
Key risks to the outlook include macroeconomic uncertainty affecting enterprise IT budgets, potential
competitive pressure from two well-funded startups that entered the market in late 2024, and execution
risk associated with the EMEA launch. The leadership team remains

with the EMEA launch. The leadership team remains confident in the company's
competitive positioning and believes the investments made in product and platform during FY2024
provide a strong foundation for sustainable growth.
This report contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of applicable securities laws. Actual results may differ
materially from those projected. Acme Corp assumes no obligation to update forward-looking statements except as required by
law.
